text.title: Technical Debt Examples
text.summary: Two worked examples — legacy API dual-maintenance and missing test coverage — demonstrating how to apply the scoring formula to reach a concrete paydown decision.

example.note: Old API v1 still maintained alongside v2 with 20 remaining users.
Interest: 3 hours/sprint maintaining both + risk of v1 bugs affecting v2 users + cannot deprecate without a migration plan.
Interest rate: High (ongoing time tax). Effort: Medium (migration path for 20 users). Alignment: High (API work planned next quarter).
Decision: Score = High × High / Medium = prioritize in Q2 alongside planned API improvements. Lowest marginal cost because the team is already context-loaded on the API surface.

section.title: Example: Missing Test Coverage on Billing Module

example.note: Billing module has 10% test coverage. Impact: 2 hours extra manual testing per release, fear of changing billing code, a previous billing bug cost $5K.
Interest: Medium-High. Effort: High (complex business logic requires deep domain understanding). Alignment: Medium (billing feature planned but not next sprint).
Decision: Add tests as part of billing feature work — Boy Scout approach. Not standalone because alignment is medium; standalone effort is high without feature context.

antipattern.description: Ignoring debt entirely — no register, no allocation, no visibility.
antipattern.reason: Without registration, debt is invisible to stakeholders and can never be prioritized against features.

antipattern.description: All-or-nothing approach: waiting for a "debt sprint" that never gets scheduled because features always win.
antipattern.reason: Continuous small allocation (15-20% per sprint) is more durable than periodic large allocations that are always displaced by roadmap pressure.

antipattern.description: No business case for debt items — technical language only, no $ or hours quantification.
antipattern.reason: Non-technical stakeholders cannot prioritize an item described only in technical terms. Every item needs a business-impact statement (hours/sprint lost, incidents caused, features blocked).
